Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard source array with a GitHub release download URL that uses the declared pkgver variable. The source is a static, versioned tarball over HTTPS from the project's own upstream repository. The sha256sums array contains a valid checksum matching the source, meaning any tampering would be detected during extraction (though the actual extraction happens after sourcing, the checksum presence is a good hygiene indicator).

The PKGBUILD contains no top-level command substitutions, no inline code execution, and no use of `pkgver()` function — the version is statically defined. The `package()` function is irrelevant for this gate since `--noprepare --nobuild` prevents it from running. The `$install` variable references a local file (`$pkgname.install`) which, while it could contain code, would not be executed during `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. Fetching and parsing this PKGBUILD involves no dangerous operations.

Thus, running the specified makepkg commands will safely download a verifiable upstream source and parse a clean PKGBUILD without executing arbitrary or remote code.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: No remote code execution on source fetch
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for archlinux-themes-sddm.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `archlinux-themes-sddm.install` file contains only a `post_install()` function that outputs instructional text to the user on how to configure SDDM to use one of the installed themes. It prints clear, readable configuration snippets showing how to set either "archlinux-simplyblack" or "archlinux-soft-grey" as the current theme in `/etc/sddm.conf`. 

There are no suspicious operations present, such as file modifications, network calls, command execution via eval or system, or data exfiltration. The file solely serves an informative purpose, which is a common and accepted practice in `.install` files for AUR packages. It does not alter system configuration automatically, nor does it rely on obfuscated or dangerous commands.

This behavior aligns with standard AUR packaging conventions and poses no security risk.
</details>
<summary>
Informs user of config steps; no active changes or malware
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is a standard Arch Linux package build script for `archlinux-themes-sddm`, a collection of SDDM login screen themes adapted from KDM. It declares a single source from the project's official GitHub release URL, using a fixed version tag (v2.0) and a matching, verifiable sha256 checksum. The build process (`package()`) safely copies pre-existing theme directories (archlinux-*) into the appropriate system directory under `${pkgdir}` with proper permissions.

There are no suspicious operations such as dynamic downloads at build time, use of `eval`, obfuscated commands, or execution of external scripts. The `source` array points to a legitimate release tarball, and the checksum ensures integrity. The operations are limited to file copying and permission setting—routine actions for a themes package. No network access or dangerous system modifications occur during packaging.

Maintainer metadata and licensing appear legitimate, and dependencies are minimal and expected (only `sddm`). The package follows standard AUR practices without red flags.
</details>
<summary>
Standard theme package with no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `.nvchecker.toml` is a configuration file for the `nvchecker` tool, which is used to check for new upstream versions of software. It defines a git source pointing to the package's own upstream repository on GitHub (`https://github.com/Guidobelix/archlinux-themes-sddm.git`) with a version prefix `"v"`. This is a standard and expected use of nvchecker in AUR packaging. There is no obfuscated code, no suspicious network requests to unrelated hosts, no system-modifying operations, and no execution of untrusted content. The file is entirely benign and performs no actions by itself; it only serves as metadata for a version-checking tool.
</details>
<summary>

LLM auditresponse for Main.qml:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This is a standard SDDM theme QML file for the **archlinux-themes-sddm** package. It defines a login screen UI using Qt Quick and SDDM components. There is no obfuscated code, no network requests, no base64 decoding, no `eval`, and no unusual file operations. The only external references are to local images (`archlinux.png` and `angle-down.png`) and a background image from the theme's configuration (`config.background`), both of which are normal for SDDM themes. The file interacts with the SDDM interface in expected ways: handling login, power off, and reboot. No malicious behavior is present.
</details>
<summary>Standard SDDM login theme; no malicious code found.</summary>
</security_assessment>

data.reasoning: Both audits confirm the package contains only static theme files and standard metadata. PKGBUILD uses verified HTTPS source with checksum, and .install script is informational-only with no executable logic. QML/config files show no malicious behavior.
coverage_issues: qwen model failed to review source tarball contents (QML/config files) which execute in SDDM context
Deepseek skipped binary tarball without extracting contents for review
false_negatives: qwen model missed reviewing QML files (runtime-executed code) due to shallow coverage
learnings: Prioritize extraction/review of source archives containing runtime-executed files (QML/scripts)
Enforce review of all files referenced in PKGBUILD install steps
Improve handling of binary files: auto-extract common formats for content review
Adjust file selection heuristics to prioritize runtime artifacts over metadata (e.g. QML > LICENSE)
